file_name: Gola et al. - 2023 - Automated melanoma skin disease detection using the spike herd search algorithm.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses the Spiking Herd Search Learning (SHSL) algorithm, a hybrid metaheuristic approach combining Horse Herd Optimisation (HOA) and Squirrel Search Optimisation (SSO), to optimize feature selection for classification using Spiking Neural Networks (SNN) and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN). This AI/ML method was applied to the DermIS Image dataset for automated melanoma skin disease detection, achieving 98.91% accuracy.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a novel 'Spiking Herd Search Learning (SHSL)' algorithm for automated melanoma detection. It details specific methodology including preprocessing, segmentation, feature selection using hybrid metaheuristic algorithms (HOA and SSO), and classification using SNN and CNN. The study reports original results, including a 98.91% accuracy on the DermIS Image dataset, and compares these findings with existing methods, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: Automated Melanoma Skin Disease Detection Using the Spike Herd Search Algorithm
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of automated melanoma skin disease detection by proposing the Spiking Herd Search Learning (SHSL) algorithm, a hybrid metaheuristic approach combining Horse Herd Optimisation (HOA) and Squirrel Search Optimisation (SSO). The methodology involves preprocessing with Gaussian filtering, segmentation via k-means clustering, and feature selection using the hybrid SHSL algorithm to optimize inputs for Spiking Neural Network (SNN) and Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) classifiers. The proposed model was evaluated using the DermIS Image dataset, which comprised 500 malignant and 500 benign images. Performance was assessed against five conventional methods using metrics including specificity, sensitivity, precision, and accuracy. The SHSL algorithm achieved superior results with 99.13% specificity, 99.29% sensitivity, 99.42% precision, and 98.91% accuracy, outperforming existing approaches.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target of the automated detection system. The DermIS Image dataset used in the experiments contained 500 malignant images.

Used as the negative class for binary classification. The DermIS Image dataset contained 500 benign images. The paper notes that distinguishing between malignant melanoma and benign lesions is a key challenge.

all_task_types.details: Binary classification of skin lesions into malignant (melanoma) and benign categories using the SHSL algorithm with SNN and CNN classifiers on the DermIS Image dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic labels (malignant/benign) and achieves 98.91% accuracy, 99.13% specificity, and 99.29% sensitivity.

datasets_used.name: DermIS Image dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset obtained from Kaggle (https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/farhatullah8398/skin-lesion-dermis-dataset) used for automated melanoma skin disease detection.

use.training: Used for training the Spiking Herd Search Learning (SHSL) algorithm with Spiking Neural Network (SNN) and Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) classifiers.
use.testing: Used for evaluating the performance of the proposed model against five conventional methods using metrics including specificity, sensitivity, precision, and accuracy.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (accuracy, specificity, sensitivity, precision) and compared qualitatively to other methods.

DermIS Image dataset.notes: The SHSL algorithm outperformed all five conventional methods (CNN-TL, WHO, GO-GOEGCN, CNFM-MOA, DCNN-AFSM) across all reported metrics. The dataset consisted of 500 malignant and 500 benign images.

architecture_used.main_model: Spiking Herd Search Learning (SHSL) algorithm, a hybrid metaheuristic approach combining Horse Herd Optimisation (HOA) and Squirrel Search Optimisation (SSO) for feature selection, used with Spiking Neural Network (SNN) and Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) classifiers.

architecture_details.feature_selection: Hybrid metaheuristic algorithm combining Horse Herd Optimisation (HOA) and Squirrel Search Optimisation (SSO).
architecture_details.preprocessing: Gaussian filter for noise removal, Greyscale conversion, and K-means clustering for segmentation.
architecture_details.feature_extraction: Grey-level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) used to record positional interdependence among pixel intensities.
architecture_details.snn_details: Weights adjusted using gradient descent for classification based on selected features.
architecture_details.cnn_details: Evaluated as a baseline method (CNN-TL) using transfer learning.

training_details.optimizer: Gradient descent (for SNN weight adjustment); Hybrid HOA-SSO metaheuristic algorithms (for feature selection optimization).
training_details.hyperparameters: Seasonal constant (SEct) for SSO; Equilibrium glide (EG) parameters for HOA; Number of squirrels (n1, n2, n3) for SSO phases.

classification_or_output_head.details: Outputs skin disease recognition label based on weighted melanoma skin disease features.

preprocessing_techniques: Random over-sampling for handling imbalanced data

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used to handle imbalanced data in the DermIS Image dataset (500 malignant and 500 benign images) before training.
Applied following random over-sampling to increase efficiency and eliminate inappropriate matching.

conclusions: The proposed Spiking Herd Search Learning (SHSL) algorithm, a hybrid metaheuristic approach combining Horse Herd Optimisation (HOA) and Squirrel Search Optimisation (SSO), effectively addresses the challenges of automated melanoma skin disease detection.
The SHSL method achieved a classification accuracy of 98.91% on the DermIS Image dataset, outperforming five conventional methods (CNN-TL, WHO, GO-GOEGCN, CNFM-MOA, and DCNN-AFSM).
The SHSL algorithm demonstrated superior performance across multiple metrics, achieving 99.13% specificity, 99.29% sensitivity, and 99.42% precision.

The integration of the SHSL feature selection algorithm with Spiking Neural Network (SNN) and Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) classifiers provides a robust solution for distinguishing between malignant melanoma and benign skin lesions.

The study highlights that conventional deep learning methods often suffer from poor generalization, overfitting, and high dependency on iteration and parameter adjustments, which the proposed SHSL approach aims to mitigate.
important_remarks: The study relies on a small, balanced dataset (DermIS Image dataset) with only 1000 total images (500 malignant, 500 benign), which limits the generalizability of the results to larger, more diverse clinical populations.
The dataset was obtained from Kaggle, raising potential concerns regarding data provenance, quality control, and the presence of selection bias compared to standardized benchmarks like ISIC.

The paper does not report any statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) or confidence intervals for the performance metrics, making it difficult to assess the statistical robustness of the claimed superiority over baseline methods.
There is no mention of external validation on independent datasets; the model was only evaluated on the single DermIS dataset, which is a significant limitation for clinical deployment readiness.
The paper does not explicitly detail the train/validation/test split ratios or the cross-validation strategy used, creating a risk of data leakage or overfitting to the specific dataset partition.

The comparative baseline methods (e.g., CNN-TL, WHO, GO-GOEGCN) appear to be cited from other literature rather than re-implemented and re-trained on the same data under identical conditions, which may introduce unfair comparisons due to differences in preprocessing or hyperparameter tuning.

The proposed SHSL algorithm is a hybrid metaheuristic approach for feature selection combined with SNN and CNN classifiers; however, the computational complexity and inference time required for such hybrid optimization are not discussed, which is critical for real-time clinical deployment.

The paper acknowledges that conventional deep learning methods suffer from poor generalization and overfitting, yet it does not provide evidence that the proposed method avoids these issues on unseen or noisy clinical data beyond the specific test set.
